papers.title: Effectively Closed Subgroups of the Infinite Symmetric Group
papers.why_relevant: It treats automorphism groups of computable structures as effectively closed subgroups of S_infinity and analyzes discrete/countable cases. Those tools support the finite-base reduction and possible constructions with prescribed countable automorphism groups.

lemmas.label: Proposition 1.3
lemmas.statement: Let S∞ be the group of all permutations of ω. Equip S∞ with its natural computable Polish presentation: if d is the usual ultrametric on ω^ω, set D(σ,τ) = (d(σ,τ)+d(σ^{-1},τ^{-1}))/2, and take the finite-support permutations as a computable dense set. For a subgroup G ≤ S∞, the following are equivalent: (i) G is effectively closed in the sense that there is a Π^0_1 subset P of Baire space ω^ω such that G = P ∩ S∞; (ii) G, as a subset of the computable Polish space (S∞,D), is effectively closed, i.e. the complement S∞ \ G is a computably enumerable union of D-basic open balls.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The key point is effective compatibility between the usual Baire-space metric d on ω^ω and the Polish-group metric D on S∞. Given a Baire basic open set U specified by a finite partial map σ, compute a D-open cover of U ∩ S∞. If σ is not injective then U ∩ S∞ is empty. If σ is injective, enumerate all finite injective extensions τ_i of σ^{-1} whose domains are finite initial segments of ω. The corresponding D-basic conditions, requiring a permutation to extend σ and its inverse to extend τ_i, cover exactly U ∩ S∞: every permutation extending σ has an inverse extending some τ_i, and conversely every permutation in such a D-basic set extends σ. Conversely, a D-basic open set is given by finite information about a permutation and its inverse; in the standard case this is a finite permutation σ of an initial segment, and the corresponding Baire cylinder U of all extensions of σ satisfies U ∩ S∞ equal to that D-basic set. These transformations are uniform and computable. Therefore an effective enumeration of basic open balls covering the complement in one presentation can be converted uniformly into such an enumeration in the other presentation, proving equivalence of the two Π^0_1 notions.

lemmas.label: Proposition 4.1
lemmas.statement: Let G ≤ S∞ be a discrete effectively closed subgroup. For finite tuples a,b ∈ ω^{<ω}, define a ∼_G b iff |a|=|b| and there is σ ∈ G such that σ(a)=b coordinatewise. Then the orbit equivalence relation ∼_G on ω^{<ω} is hyperarithmetic, uniformly in the tuple length.
lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 1.3, an effectively closed subgroup of S∞ is a Π^0_2 subset of Baire space ω^ω, hence analytic. Since G is discrete as a subspace of the separable Polish group S∞, it has no nonempty perfect subset. The effective perfect set theorem for Σ^1_1 sets says that an analytic set with no perfect subset consists only of hyperarithmetic elements. Spector Σ^1_1 bounding then gives a single computable ordinal γ such that every σ ∈ G is computable from 0^(γ). Using the Π^0_2 definition of G and this uniform degree bound, 0^(γ+2) computes a listing of all elements of G: enumerate all 0^(γ)-computable total functions/permutations and test the Π^0_2 membership condition with two further jumps. Given such a listing (σ_i), for finite tuples a,b of the same length, a ∼_G b iff ∃i[σ_i(a)=b]. This is Σ^0_1 relative to the listing, and its negation is Π^0_1 relative to the listing; one more jump decides it. Thus ∼_G is computable from 0^(γ+3), uniformly for all finite tuple arities, hence hyperarithmetic.

lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2
lemmas.statement: Let G ≤ S∞ be an effectively closed subgroup, and suppose its finite-tuple orbit equivalence relation ∼_G is hyperarithmetic. Then there is a computable first-order structure N, in a computable language and with domain ω, such that Aut(N) is topologically isomorphic to G. Consequently, together with Proposition 4.1, every discrete effectively closed subgroup of S∞ is topologically isomorphic to the automorphism group of some computable structure.
lemmas.proof_sketch: First build a structure M computable from ∼_G whose automorphism group is exactly G. Use a computable relational language with, for each finite tuple a ∈ ω^n, an n-ary relation symbol R_a, interpreted by R_a(x) iff x ∼_G a. Duplicated orbit relations are harmless and avoid choosing representatives. Every σ ∈ G preserves all R_a, so G ≤ Aut(M). Conversely, if f ∈ Aut(M), then for every finite tuple a, M ⊨ R_a(f(a)), hence a ∼_G f(a), so some g_a ∈ G agrees with f on a. Thus every finite restriction of f is realized by an element of G; since G is closed in S∞, f ∈ G. Hence Aut(M)=G, and M is hyperarithmetic because ∼_G is. Next use the preservation lemma for hyperarithmetic structures: if M is Δ^0_γ for a computable ordinal γ, replace each relation value by a computable rigid linear-order gadget. More explicitly, first make the language relational. For each relation symbol P of arity n and tuple a ∈ M^n, add a disjoint fiber C^P_a linked to the base tuple a by a computable incidence relation. Put on C^P_a one of two uniformly computable rigid linear orders according as P(a) holds or fails. The required uniform coding uses the Goncharov-Knight/Marker-style fact that membership in a Σ^0_γ (hence Δ^0_γ, by coding true and false cases) set can be uniformly represented by choosing between two rigid computable linear orders, e.g. suitable computable copies of ordinal orders such as ω^{γ+1} and ω^{γ+2}. The resulting expanded structure N is computable. Rigidity of the fibers implies every automorphism of N is determined by its restriction to the original M-sort, and the order type of each fiber forces that restriction to preserve exactly the relations of M. Conversely, every automorphism of M extends uniquely to N by the unique order isomorphisms between corresponding fibers. The restriction map Aut(N) → Aut(M) is therefore a group isomorphism. It is topological: finite constraints on base elements are preserved directly, while a finite constraint mentioning a fiber element q ∈ C^P_a maps, by rigidity, to a finite constraint on the image of the base tuple a, with either a uniquely determined compatible fiber image or no compatible automorphism. Thus Aut(N) ≅ Aut(M)=G as topological groups.

papers.title: Degree Spectra of Relations on a Cone
papers.why_relevant: It gives the main bridge between intrinsic computability on a cone and infinitary definability of relations, exactly the kind of theorem needed for the automorphism-graph relation. Its free-tuple and coding arguments are also directly relevant for contrapositives.

lemmas.label: Theorem 2.5 plus Section 2.3 cone consequence
lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable first-order structure and let R ⊆ A^n be an invariant relation, meaning that every automorphism of A fixes R setwise, equivalently R has an L_{ω1ω}-definition in A. For a Turing degree d, say that R is intrinsically Σ^0_α relative to d if, for every copy B ≅ A whose atomic diagram is computable in d, the induced copy R^B is a Σ^0_α(d) subset of B^n. Then, after restricting to a sufficiently high Turing cone, R is intrinsically Σ^0_α relative to d for all d on that cone if and only if R is definable in A by an infinitary Σ^in_α formula of L_{ω1ω}, allowing finitely many parameters from A. More explicitly, in the computable/relative form used to obtain this: if A is computable and α is a computable ordinal, then R is relatively intrinsically Σ^0_α, i.e. in every copy B of A the relation R^B is Σ^0_α(B), if and only if R is definable in A by a computable infinitary Σ^c_α formula with finitely many parameters. The analogous Π statement is obtained by applying the result to the complement, and intrinsic computability/Δ^0_α on a cone is equivalent to having both Σ^in_α and Π^in_α definitions.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward computable-relative equivalence is the Ash–Knight–Manasse–Slaman/Chisholm theorem. The easy direction is evaluation: if R is defined by a computable infinitary Σ^c_α formula with finitely many parameters, then in any copy B, after naming the corresponding parameters, satisfaction of that formula is a Σ^0_α(B) condition. For the hard direction, the AKMS/Chisholm argument uses back-and-forth relations and computable infinitary types: if no computable Σ^c_α formula separates tuples in R from tuples outside R, one builds/codes a copy in which the image of R fails to be Σ^0_α relative to the diagram, contradicting relative intrinsic Σ^0_α-ness. The proof avoids the Ash–Nerode effectivity hypothesis by allowing the oracle to be the whole diagram of the copy. To pass to cones, relativize the theorem to an oracle computing the relevant presentation of A, the relation R, the finite parameters, and the infinitary formula/ordinal notation. Above that cone, all objects that had to be computable in the relative theorem become computable in the cone oracle. Thus the computability restriction on the defining formula disappears and one obtains exactly Σ^in_α-definability. For α = 1, which is the level most relevant for graphs of computable functions, a Σ^in_1 definition gives an enumeration of the graph in every copy; if the graph is total functional, this enumeration computes the function by searching for the unique output.

lemmas.label: Corollary 2.7 (Harizanov on a cone)
lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and R an invariant relation on A. For a Turing degree d, let dgSp(A,R)≤d be the set of degrees deg(R^B ⊕ D), where D is any representative of d, B ranges over copies of A with atomic diagram B ≤T D, and R^B is the induced copy of R in B. Let dgSprel(A,R) denote the resulting degree spectrum on a cone, i.e. the equivalence class of the map d ↦ dgSp(A,R)≤d modulo equality on a cone. Then exactly one of the following holds on a cone: (1) dgSprel(A,R) = Δ^0_1, meaning that for all sufficiently high d and all B ≅ A with B ≤T d, R^B is computable in d; or (2) dgSprel(A,R) ⊇ Σ^0_1, meaning that for all sufficiently high d and every set E c.e. in d, there is a copy B ≅ A with B ≤T d such that R^B ⊕ d ≡T E ⊕ d. The cone may be taken above (A ⊕ R)′′, using fixed codes for the diagrams of A and R.
lemmas.proof_sketch: This is Harizanov’s theorem after relativization. In the unrelativized effective theorem, if A is computable, R is computable, R is not relatively intrinsically computable, and a concrete effectiveness condition holds, then every c.e. degree is realized as the degree of R in some computable copy of A. The construction uses the failure of intrinsic computability to find tuples that can be moved between R and its complement while preserving finite existential information; then it codes a chosen c.e. set by changing the presentation at enumeration stages. On a cone, the effectiveness condition is automatic: an oracle above (A ⊕ R)′′ can decide the finite search questions and find all witnesses required by Harizanov’s construction. Relativizing the construction to a degree d on that cone gives: if some copy has R^B noncomputable relative to d, then every degree c.e. in and above d is realized by R in a d-computable copy. If no such noncomputable relative copy exists, all copies have R^B ≤T d and the spectrum is Δ^0_1. Thus a relation on a cone has no intermediate nontrivial behavior below the c.e. degrees: non-intrinsic computability immediately yields all c.e. degrees.

lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4, with Propositions 3.5 and 3.9
lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and R ⊆ A^m an invariant relation. For finite tuples c and a from A, say that a is d-free over c if the following holds. For every tuple b and every finitary existential formula φ(c,u,v) true of c,a,b in A, there exist tuples a′,b′ satisfying φ(c,u,v) such that the truth values of R on tuples drawn from c,a′ are not the same as the truth values of R on the corresponding tuples drawn from c,a, and such that, for every finitary existential formula ψ(c,u,v) true of c,a′,b′, there exist tuples a′′,b′′ satisfying ψ(c,u,v) for which the truth values of R on all tuples drawn from c,a′′,b′′ are exactly the same as the truth values of R on the corresponding tuples drawn from c,a,b. If for every finite tuple c there is a tuple a that is d-free over c, then the degree spectrum dgSprel(A,R) on a cone strictly contains the c.e. degrees: on a sufficiently high cone it contains all c.e. degrees and also contains a non-c.e. degree. Conversely, if R is formally d.c.e. — i.e. on the cone R is definable as a difference of two Σ^in_1-definable relations, equivalently by a d.c.e. infinitary definition — then the existence of such d-free tuples over every c is necessary for the spectrum on a cone to strictly contain the c.e. degrees. Equivalently, for formally d.c.e. R, if some tuple c has no d-free tuple over it, then in every copy on a sufficiently high cone the induced relation R^B has c.e. degree relative to the base oracle.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The sufficiency direction is Proposition 3.9 relativized to a cone. In the computable version, assume effective access to d-free tuples and witnesses. Build a computable copy B by finite-injury partial isomorphisms Fs: initial segments of ω are mapped into A, and the diagram of B is the pullback limit. Requirements Re,i,j diagonalize against every alleged equivalence R^B = Φ_i^{W_e} and W_e = Φ_j^{R^B}, where W_e is c.e. A requirement chooses a fresh d-free tuple a over the current finite range and places it so that initially its R-pattern has one value. After computations R^B[≤n] = Φ_i^{W_e}[≤n] with use u and W_e[≤u] = Φ_j^{R^B}[≤u] with use v appear, d-freeness lets the construction replace a by a′ with the opposite relevant R-pattern while preserving the finite existential diagram, breaking the first computation. If W_e later changes below u, the second half of d-freeness supplies a′′ and witnesses restoring the old R-pattern below v while preserving all finite facts already committed; then the second computation is wrong because W_e changed but the R^B-oracle segment used by Φ_j was restored. Lower-priority strategies are injured; finite injury proves every requirement is met, so R^B is not of c.e. degree. Harizanov’s cone corollary then supplies all c.e. degrees as well, hence strict containment. The necessity direction is Proposition 3.5. If a tuple c has no d-free tuple and R is formally d.c.e., write R as α ∧ ¬β with α,β existential infinitary. In any copy B, define a c.e. set A of elements/tuples that appear to be in R at two stages and pass all finitely many non-d-freeness obstruction tests witnessed by formulas φ and ψ. One shows R^B ⊆ A, A − R^B is c.e. via β, and A is computable from R^B because a failed obstruction can be recognized using the actual R^B-pattern. Hence R^B ≡T A ⊕ (A − R^B), a join of c.e. sets, so R^B has c.e. degree. On a cone, the searches for the obstruction witnesses are computable, giving the stated relativized necessity.

papers.title: A Robuster Scott Rank
papers.why_relevant: Its type-omitting lemmas for unsupported infinitary types give a natural contrapositive strategy: if an automorphism graph is not existentially definable over the prescribed parameters, build a copy where computability fails.

lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2 (Type omitting lemma)
lemmas.statement: Let A be a structure in a countable vocabulary, let α be a countable ordinal, and let σ be a Π^in_{α+1} sentence such that A ⊨ σ. Let Φ(x) be a partial Π^in_α-type, i.e. a set of Π^in_α formulas in the finite tuple of variables x. Say that Φ is Σ^in_α-supported in A if there is a Σ^in_α formula θ(x) such that A ⊨ ∃x θ(x), and for every ψ(x) ∈ Φ, A ⊨ ∀x(θ(x) → ψ(x)). If Φ is not Σ^in_α-supported in A, then there is a countable structure B in the same vocabulary such that B ⊨ σ and B omits Φ, meaning that no tuple b from B satisfies every formula in Φ.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Put σ in normal form as a countable conjunction ∧_j ∀y_j σ_j(y_j), with each σ_j a Σ^in_α formula. Add fresh constants C = {c_0,c_1,...}. Build, by a Henkin construction, an increasing sequence of finite sets S_s of sentences in the expanded language, and assignments v_s of the constants mentioned in S_s into A, such that every sentence in S_s is true in A under v_s. Requirements scheduled over ω ensure: disjunctions in S have a chosen disjunct in S; existential formulas in S get witnesses among constants; conjunctions are decomposed; universals are instantiated at all constant tuples; every atomic sentence is decided consistently; for each j and tuple c, σ_j(c) is put into S; and for every tuple c of constants of the same length as x, some negation ¬ψ(c), ψ ∈ Φ, is put into S. The routine Henkin requirements preserve satisfiability in A by choosing the disjunct, witness, instance, or atomic truth that holds under the current assignment. The key type-omitting step is: given a tuple c and current finite S_s, let d be the other constants in S_s and let η(c,d) be the finite conjunction of S_s. Then ∃y η(x,y) is a Σ^in_α formula realized in A. Since Φ is not supported, this formula cannot imply all of Φ in A; hence for some ψ ∈ Φ there are a,b in A with A ⊨ η(a,b) ∧ ¬ψ(a). Add ¬ψ(c) and reset the assignment on c,d to a,b. Let S be the union. The complete consistent atomic part of S gives the Henkin/term model B, quotienting by equality if necessary. Induction on formula complexity using the decomposition and witness requirements shows every sentence in S holds in B. The σ_j(c) requirements yield B ⊨ σ, and the type-omitting requirements ensure each tuple of constants, hence each tuple of B, fails some formula from Φ.

lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1 and its continuation
lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let α < ω_1. For finite tuples u,v from A, write u ≤_γ v if every Π^in_γ formula true of u in A is true of v. A tuple a is α-free if for every β < α and every finite tuple d from A, there are tuples a′,d′ from A such that a d ≤_β a′ d′ but a is not ≤_α a′. A Π^in_α-type p(x) realized in A is Σ^in_α-supported within A if some Σ^in_α formula θ(x) is realized in A and implies, within A, every formula in p. Then the following are equivalent: (U1) for every finite tuple a from A, its automorphism orbit {b : some automorphism of A sends a to b} is definable in A without parameters by a Σ^in_α formula; (U2) A has a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence, i.e. a Π^in_{α+1} sentence whose countable models are exactly the copies of A; (U3) A is uniformly boldface Δ^0_α-categorical, i.e. after fixing a presentation of A there is a boldface Δ^0_α operator Θ such that for every presentation B of A, Θ(B) is an isomorphism from A onto B; (U4) the set of presentations of A, viewed as a subset of the standard Polish space of countable structures with domain ω in the given vocabulary, is Π^0_{α+1} in the Borel hierarchy; (U5) every Π^in_α-type realized in A is Σ^in_α-supported within A; (U6) there is a Π^in_{α+1} sentence χ true of A such that every structure B with B ⊨ χ satisfies B ≡_{α+1} A, equivalently B and A satisfy the same Σ^in_{α+1} sentences or are (α+1)-back-and-forth equivalent; (U7) no tuple in A is α-free.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The infinitary-logic core is U1⇒U2⇒U6⇒U5⇒U1. For U1⇒U2, let φ_a be a Σ^in_α definition of the orbit of each tuple a. The standard Scott-family sentence is the conjunction, over all tuples a, of ∀x[φ_a(x) → ((∧_{b∈A} ∃y φ_ab(x,y)) ∧ ∀y ∨_{b∈A} φ_ab(x,y))]. It says that every tuple in the orbit of a has exactly the possible one-point extensions appearing in A. Counting quantifier alternations gives a Π^in_{α+1} sentence. U2⇒U6 is immediate, taking the Scott sentence. For U6⇒U5, suppose p is a realized Π^in_α-type not Σ^in_α-supported. Lemma 3.2 applied to the Π^in_{α+1} sentence χ gives B ⊨ χ omitting p. By U6, B ≡_{α+1} A. Since B is countable, choose for each tuple of B a formula from p it fails; the resulting countable q ⊆ p is omitted in B. If a realizes p in A, then A ⊨ ∃x ∧_{ψ∈q} ψ(x), while B satisfies its negation. This is a Σ^in_{α+1} distinction, contradiction. For U5⇒U1, for each tuple a let φ_a support the Π^in_α-type of a. First φ_a(a) holds, since otherwise ¬φ_a would be in the type of a and would be implied by φ_a. Also, if A ⊨ φ_a(b), then A ⊨ φ_b(a); otherwise ¬φ_b is in the Π^in_α-type of a, so φ_a implies ¬φ_b, contradicting φ_a(b) and φ_b(b). Let P = {(a,b): A ⊨ φ_a(b)}. This relation has the back-and-forth property: if (a,b) ∈ P and d ∈ A, assume no c has φ_{b d}(a,c). Then ∀y ¬φ_{b d}(a,y) belongs to the Π^in_α-type of a, so φ_a implies it; applying φ_a(b) gives ∀y ¬φ_{b d}(b,y), contradicting φ_{b d}(b,d). Thus choose c with φ_{b d}(a,c), and symmetry gives φ_{a c}(b,d). Back-and-forth yields an automorphism sending a to b, so φ_a defines the orbit. The remaining equivalences are imported standard facts: U2⇔U4 is López-Escobar's theorem; U1⇔U3 is the Ash/Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman characterization of uniform boldface Δ^0_α categoricity by Σ^in_α Scott families; U1⇔U7 is Ash-Knight's α-freeness characterization, relativized to a sufficiently large oracle and in the parameter-free version.

lemmas.label: Theorem 2.5 (including Theorem 2.4 as α = 1)
lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure and let α be a countable ordinal. The following are equivalent: (C1) A is Δ^0_α-categorical on a cone, meaning that there is a real Y such that for every X ≥_T Y, whenever B and C are X-computable presentations of A, there is an isomorphism B ≅ C of complexity Δ^0_α relative to X; (C2) A has a Σ^in_{α+2} Scott sentence; (C3) there is a finite tuple a from A such that the expansion (A,a) by constants naming a has categoricity Scott rank at most α, equivalently every automorphism orbit of a finite tuple in (A,a) is definable without parameters in the expanded language by a Σ^in_α formula. In particular, taking α = 1, A is computably categorical on a cone if and only if A has a Σ^in_3 Scott sentence.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof combines the uniform categoricity facts with Theorem 1.1 and the usual parameter trick. Ventsov's theorem, together with Ash's higher-level version, says that relative Δ^0_α-categoricity becomes uniform Δ^0_α-categoricity after naming finitely many parameters; on a cone all non-uniform choices, codes for operators, and presentations can be absorbed into the base oracle. Thus C1 is equivalent to the existence of a finite tuple a such that (A,a) is uniformly boldface Δ^0_α-categorical. By Theorem 1.1, this is equivalent to every tuple orbit in (A,a) being Σ^in_α-definable, i.e. C3, and also equivalent to (A,a) having a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence. If (A,a) has such a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence θ with constants for a, replace those constants by variables and existentially quantify them: ∃x θ(x) is a Σ^in_{α+2} Scott sentence for the reduct A, giving C2. Conversely, a Σ^in_{α+2} Scott sentence for A can be put into normal form as a countable disjunction of existential tuples followed by Π^in_{α+1} formulas. Choosing a disjunct and witnesses realized in A yields finite parameters a; the countable categoricity of the original sentence, together with the same Ventsov/Ash parameter argument, gives a Π^in_{α+1} Scott sentence and hence uniform boldface Δ^0_α-categoricity for (A,a). Applying Theorem 1.1 gives C3. For α = 1, Δ^0_1 relative to X is X-computability, so C1 is precisely computable categoricity on a cone and C2 is the Σ^in_3 Scott-sentence condition of Theorem 2.4.

papers.title: Computable Functors and Effective Interpretability
papers.why_relevant: It contains effective interpretability and intrinsic definability machinery that can convert uniform relative c.e. behavior into computable infinitary existential definitions. This is central to proving a global action relation is Sigma-in-1 definable.

lemmas.label: AKMS–Chisholm intrinsic definability characterization, quoted after Definition 1
lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure in a computable language, and let R⊆A^{<ω}. Say that R is uniformly relatively intrinsically computably enumerable, u.r.i.c.e., if there is a c.e. operator W such that for every copy (B,R^B) of (A,R), where B has domain ω and D(B) is its atomic diagram, R^B = W^{D(B)}. Say that R is uniformly relatively intrinsically computable, u.r.i. computable, if there is a computable operator Ψ such that for every copy (B,R^B) of (A,R), R^B = Ψ^{D(B)}. Then R is u.r.i.c.e. if and only if R is definable in A, without parameters, by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas: for R⊆A^{<ω}, this means that there is a computable list φ_1,φ_2,φ_3,... of Σ^c_1 formulas such that φ_n defines R∩A^n. Moreover, R is u.r.i. computable if and only if both R and its complement are definable without parameters by computably infinitary Σ^c_1 formulas, equivalently R is Δ^c_1-definable. The same convention applies uniformly to a sequence of relations (R_i : i∈ω), R_i⊆A^{<ω}: the sequence is u.r.i.c.e. or Σ^c_1-definable when the associated subset of A^{<ω}×ω, using an effective coding of A^{<ω}×ω into A^{<ω}, has that property.
lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Ash–Knight–Manasse–Slaman and Chisholm for the finite-arity theorem and Mon12 for the extension to A^{<ω}. The easy direction is that a computable infinitary existential definition can be enumerated from the atomic diagram of any copy: enumerate the computable disjunctions and all finite witness tuples, and use D(B) to check the quantifier-free atomic diagram requirements. If both R and its complement have such enumerations, run them in parallel to compute the characteristic function. Conversely, from a uniform c.e. operator W, build a Σ^c_1 definition by listing every finite partial atomic diagram δ, with distinguished free tuple positions, such that W with oracle δ already enumerates the corresponding tuple. Convert δ into a finitary existential formula saying that the free tuple, together with some extra variables, realizes δ. The disjunction over all such finite successful computations is computable. Correctness uses intrinsicity: if a tuple in A satisfies one of these finite diagrams, extend that finite configuration to a copy B of A; the same finite oracle computation makes W enumerate the tuple, so it is in R. Conversely, if a tuple is in R, put it into the distinguished positions in some copy; W enumerates it after finite use, producing one of the disjuncts. The A^{<ω} and sequence versions are obtained by the paper’s effective coding of variable-length tuples and the extra index i into tuples from A.

lemmas.label: Theorem 5 with the Proposition 14 construction
lemmas.statement: Let A=(A;P_0^A,P_1^A,...) and B be countable structures in computable relational languages, with P_i of computable arity p(i). Let Iso(A) and Iso(B) be the categories whose objects are copies of A and B with domain ω and whose morphisms are isomorphisms. A computable functor F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) is a functor together with Turing operators Φ and Φ_* such that, for every copy B̃ of B, Φ^{D(B̃)} is the atomic diagram of F(B̃), and for every isomorphism h:B̃→B̂, Φ_*^{D(B̃)⊕h⊕D(B̂)} is the isomorphism F(h):F(B̃)→F(B̂). An effective interpretation of A in B consists of parameter-free Δ^c_1-definable relations Dom_B^A⊆B^{<ω}, an equivalence relation ∼ on Dom_B^A, and relations R_i⊆(B^{<ω})^{p(i)} closed under ∼, uniformly in i, such that (Dom_B^A/∼;R_0/∼,R_1/∼,...) is isomorphic to A. Then A is effectively interpretable in B if and only if there exists a computable functor Iso(B)→Iso(A). In the nontrivial direction, given F=(Φ,Φ_*), the interpretation may be chosen explicitly as follows, after coding B^{<ω}×ω into B^{<ω} and after identifying A with the copy F(B_0) for a fixed presentation B_0 of B, or composing with any fixed isomorphism F(B_0)≅A. For a tuple b̄∈B^n, let D(b̄) be the finite atomic diagram of the first n elements in the pullback copy determined by any bijection f:ω→B extending b̄, restricted as in the paper to the first n relation symbols, and let λ be the identity map on ω. Put (b̄,i) in Dom_B^A iff Φ_*^{D(b̄)⊕(λ↾n)⊕D(b̄)}(i) converges with value i. For (b̄,i),(c̄,j)∈Dom_B^A, let c̄′ list the elements of c̄ not occurring in b̄ and b̄′ list the elements of b̄ not occurring in c̄. Declare (b̄,i)∼(c̄,j) iff there is a finite tuple d̄ of elements not occurring in b̄ or c̄ and a finite permutation σ with (b̄ c̄′ d̄)=(c̄ b̄′ d̄)σ such that Φ_*^{D(b̄ c̄′ d̄)⊕σ⊕D(c̄ b̄′ d̄)}(i)=j and Φ_*^{D(c̄ b̄′ d̄)⊕σ^{-1}⊕D(b̄ c̄′ d̄)}(j)=i. Finally, for P_k of arity p, put ((b̄_1,i_1),...,(b̄_p,i_p)) in R_k iff there are a tuple c̄ and numbers j_1,...,j_p such that (b̄_s,i_s)∼(c̄,j_s) for all s≤p and the finite output Φ^{D(c̄)} already asserts the atomic fact P_k(j_1,...,j_p). These Dom_B^A, ∼, and R_k are uniformly Δ^c_1-definable without parameters and yield an effective interpretation of A in B.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Interpretation to functor is direct. From D(B̃), the Δ^c_1 definitions compute the interpreted domain, equivalence relation, and relations. Choose a canonical D(B̃)-computable bijection τ^{B̃}:ω→Dom_{B̃}^A/∼ and pull the interpreted relations back to ω to form F(B̃). For an isomorphism h:B̃→B̂, extend h to tuples and set F(h)=(τ^{B̂})^{-1}∘h∘τ^{B̃}; this is computable and functorial. Conversely, given Φ,Φ_*, a pair (b̄,i) is intended to represent the i-th element of the copy Φ^{D(B^f)} whenever f extends b̄; the identity condition in Dom forces i to be stable under changing the unused part of f. The relation ∼ says that finite information already makes Φ_* send i to j between two such pullback copies. Dom and its complement are Σ^c_1 because convergence, wrong output, and finite-oracle divergence are detected from finite diagrams. For ∼, define a Σ^c_1 relation ≁ by the same kind of finite witness but with output not equal to the required value. Totality of Φ_* on a genuine total isomorphism extending the finite permutation shows that either ∼ or ≁ holds. They cannot both hold: extend the two finite witnesses to bijections f_1,g_1,f_2,g_2 onto B; the resulting square of copies gives a factorization of one isomorphism through the others, and functoriality F(u∘v)=F(u)∘F(v), together with the Dom identity computations, would force the same value and the different value simultaneously. Thus ∼ and its complement are Σ^c_1. Symmetry is built in, reflexivity comes from Dom, and transitivity is proved by composing finite witnesses through suitable pullback copies and applying functoriality. The definitions of R_k and of its complement Q_k, obtained by requiring ¬P_k, are Σ^c_1. They are complements because any tuple can be moved to a common initial-segment representative, and if both P_k and ¬P_k were witnessed, the isomorphism between the corresponding functor outputs would both preserve and fail to preserve P_k. Finally, every class has an initial-segment representative, and if b̄⊆c̄ then (b̄,i)∼(c̄,j) iff i=j. Hence i↦(B_0↾n,i), for the least n with (B_0↾n,i)∈Dom, induces a bijective relation-preserving map from F(B_0) onto the quotient.

lemmas.label: Proposition 7
lemmas.statement: Let F:Iso(B)→Iso(A) be a computable functor. Apply the construction of Proposition 14 to F to obtain an effective interpretation of A in B, and then apply the construction of Proposition 13 to that interpretation to obtain a computable functor I_F:Iso(B)→Iso(A). Then F and I_F are effectively naturally isomorphic. That is, there exists a computable Turing functional Λ such that for every copy B̃ of B, Λ^{D(B̃)} is an isomorphism Λ_{B̃}:F(B̃)→I_F(B̃), and for every isomorphism h:B̃→B̂ the naturality equation I_F(h)∘Λ_{B̃}=Λ_{B̂}∘F(h) holds.
lemmas.proof_sketch: For each copy B̃, Proposition 14 and Lemma 22 give a uniformly D(B̃)-computable isomorphism F_{B̃}:F(B̃)→Dom_{B̃}^A/∼, sending an element i of F(B̃) to its canonical least initial-segment representative in the interpreted quotient. Proposition 13, applied to this interpretation, constructs I_F(B̃) by choosing a uniformly computable bijection τ^{B̃}:ω→Dom_{B̃}^A/∼ and pulling the interpreted structure back along τ^{B̃}; thus τ^{B̃} is an isomorphism I_F(B̃)→Dom_{B̃}^A/∼. Define Λ_{B̃}=(τ^{B̃})^{-1}∘F_{B̃}. This is uniformly computable from D(B̃). To prove naturality, let h:B̃→B̂ be an isomorphism and take i∈F(B̃), with j=F(h)(i). Suppose F_{B̃}(i)=(ā,i) and F_{B̂}(j)=(b̄,j) after possibly using longer initial segments. Since Φ_*^{D(B̃)⊕h⊕D(B̂)}(i)=j, enlarge ā and b̄ until this computation is witnessed by finite oracles; then the definition of ∼ in B̂ gives h(ā,i)∼(b̄,j). Hence the square involving F(h), h on interpreted domains, and the maps F_{B̃},F_{B̂} commutes modulo ∼. The square involving h, τ^{B̃},τ^{B̂}, and I_F(h) commutes by the definition of I_F(h). Combining the two squares gives I_F(h)∘Λ_{B̃}=Λ_{B̂}∘F(h).

papers.title: Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures
papers.why_relevant: The finite-use and permutation-extraction arguments, especially around Lemma 6.2, are highly portable to turning relative computations of automorphisms into existential support formulas. It is one of the most directly relevant sources for eliminating hidden nonuniformity.

lemmas.label: Lemma 6.2
lemmas.statement: Let A be a computable countable structure. Say that A is effectively ∃-atomic if it has a computably enumerable Scott family consisting of existential first-order formulas: a c.e. set S of existential formulas such that every finite tuple from A satisfies some formula in S, and any two tuples satisfying the same formula from S are in the same automorphism orbit of A. Say that A is uniformly effectively 1-prime if there is a Turing functional Φ such that, for every computable model B of the ∀2-theory of A, Φ with oracle the atomic diagram D(B) computes a 1-embedding from A into B, where a 1-embedding is an embedding preserving universal formulas on tuples. Then A is effectively ∃-atomic if and only if A is uniformly effectively 1-prime.
lemmas.proof_sketch: Forward direction: from a c.e. existential Scott family, uniformly build a 1-embedding f:A→B for any computable B satisfying the ∀2-theory of A. Enumerate A as a0,a1,.... At stage s, find an existential Scott-family formula φs satisfied by (a0,...,as). Given f(a0),...,f(as−1) already chosen satisfying φs−1, use that A satisfies the ∀2 sentence ∀x0...xs−1(φs−1(x0,...,xs−1) → ∃xs φs(x0,...,xs)); hence B satisfies it, so a witness for f(as) exists and can be found by search relative to D(B). If θ is universal and A⊨θ(a0,...,as), then A⊨∀x(φs(x)→θ(x)), another ∀2 fact, so B⊨θ(f(a0),...,f(as)); thus f is a 1-embedding. Reverse direction: assume Φ is uniform. The key finite-use/permutation extraction is as follows. Fix a finite tuple u from A and run Φ^{D(A)}(u), obtaining an output tuple v; since the output is a 1-embedding A→A, u and v have the same universal type. Choose s large enough that this computation has halted, uses only D(A)↾s, and the finitely many natural numbers occurring in v are among the first s positions. Form an existential formula θu(x) saying that x can be placed in the coordinate positions occupied by v inside a tuple of length s whose finite atomic diagram is exactly D(A)↾s; formally, existentially quantify the other coordinates and assert the corresponding finite atomic diagram. If a tuple b in A satisfies θu, with witnesses d for the other coordinates, make a new presentation Ã of A by a finite permutation so that the first s presented elements are arranged as in the witnessing tuple, with b in the positions corresponding to v. Then D(Ã)↾s=D(A)↾s, so the same finite computation forces Φ^{D(Ã)}(u) to output the same coordinate tuple, which now denotes b. Because Φ^{D(Ã)} is a 1-embedding, b has the same universal type as u. Hence θu supports the universal type of u. The paper phrases the same maneuver by choosing a preimage of a target initial segment under Φ^{D(A)} and then using the formula ∃y φ^at_{D(A)↾s}(x,y); the permutation argument is identical. Since A is computable and Φ is fixed, these supporting existential formulas are obtained uniformly effectively for all tuples. By the back-and-forth argument proving Theorem 1.2, uniformly supporting all realized universal types yields a c.e. existential Scott family.

lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2
lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure. For a tuple a from A, let orb_A(a) be its orbit under Aut(A). A is ∃-atomic if for every finite tuple a there is an existential formula φ_a(x) whose realizations in A are exactly orb_A(a). A type p(x) realized in A is ∃-supported in A if some existential formula φ(x), realized in A, implies every formula in p within A. A 1-embedding is an embedding preserving universal formulas on tuples, and A is 1-prime if every countable model of the ∀2-theory of A admits a 1-embedding of A into it. Then the following are equivalent: (A1) A is ∃-atomic. (A2) A has an infinitary Π2 Scott sentence. (A3) A is uniformly continuously categorical, i.e. there is a continuous operator which, from the atomic diagram of any copy B of A, outputs an isomorphism from B to A. (A4) Every first-order type realized in A is ∃-supported in A. (A5) Every universal type realized in A is ∃-supported in A. (A6) A is 1-prime.
lemmas.proof_sketch: A1⇒A4⇒A5 is immediate: an existential formula defining the automorphism orbit of a tuple implies every first-order, hence every universal, formula true of that tuple. The central support-to-orbit step is A5⇒A1. For each tuple a choose an existential φ_a supporting ∀-tp_A(a). First φ_a(a) must hold; otherwise ¬φ_a, a universal formula, belongs to ∀-tp_A(a), contradicting that φ_a is realized and implies the type. Define I={(a,b):A⊨φ_a(b)}. If A⊨φ_a(b), then symmetry holds: if not A⊨φ_b(a), then ¬φ_b∈∀-tp_A(a), so φ_a would imply ¬φ_b, contradicted by b. Thus I is symmetric. I has back-and-forth: atomic agreement follows from agreement of universal/quantifier-free facts; given (a,b)∈I and c∈A, if no d has (ac,bd)∈I, then ¬∃y φ_ac(b,y) is universal true of b, hence, using symmetry and support, true of a, contradicting witness c. The back condition follows symmetrically. A standard back-and-forth construction gives an automorphism taking a to any b satisfying φ_a, so φ_a defines orb_A(a). A1⇒A2 is Scott's construction specialized to existential orbit formulas: a Π2 sentence says that for every tuple satisfying its orbit formula, the induced relation I_B={(a,b):B⊨φ_a(b)} has atomic agreement plus forth and back extensions. A2⇒A1 uses the ∀-type omitting lemma: if A5 failed, a realized universal type not ∃-supported in the Π2 Scott class could be omitted by another model of the Scott sentence, impossible. A1⇒A6: enumerate A and use orbit formulas φ_s for initial segments. In any B satisfying the ∀2-theory of A, recursively choose images satisfying φ_s; existence of the next image is guaranteed by the ∀2 sentence ∀x(φ_s→∃y φ_{s+1}), and preservation of any universal θ true in A follows from the ∀2 sentence ∀x(φ_s→θ). A6⇒A1: if some realized universal type were not ∃-supported, omit it in a model B of the ∀2-theory of A; then no 1-embedding A→B could exist. A1⇔A3 is obtained by the same Scott-family/back-and-forth mechanism for continuous choice of isomorphisms, with the converse also following from the cited Effros/Melnikov-Montalbán continuous-categoricity theorem.

lemmas.label: Theorem 1.10
lemmas.statement: Let A be a countable structure. The following are equivalent. (B1) A is ∃-atomic over a finite tuple of parameters; equivalently, for some finite tuple p from A, the expansion (A,p) by constants naming p is ∃-atomic, so every orbit under automorphisms fixing p is definable by an existential formula with parameters p. (B2) A has an infinitary Σ3 Scott sentence. (B3) A is computably categorical on a cone: there is an oracle C⊆ω such that, for every X≥_T C, every X-computable copy of A is X-computably isomorphic to A.
lemmas.proof_sketch: B1⇒B2: if (A,p) is ∃-atomic, Lemma 7.1 gives a Π2 Scott sentence ψ(c) for the expanded structure with constants c naming p. Then ∃y ψ(y), with the constants replaced by variables and existentially quantified, is a Σ3 Scott sentence for the reduct A. B2⇒B1 is Lemma 7.2. Write a Σ3 Scott sentence as a countable disjunction ∨_i ∃y_i ψ_i(y_i), with each ψ_i Π2. Since A satisfies some disjunct, that disjunct alone is already a Scott sentence; write it as ∃y ψ(y). Add constants c of the same length as y and consider the Π2 class K of countable models of ψ(c). Every reduct of a member of K is isomorphic to A, because ∃y ψ(y) is Scott for A. Thus K has only countably many isomorphism types of expansions, since they correspond to tuples in copies of the countable structure A, and hence only countably many universal types realized across K. List those realized universal types that are not ∃-supported within K. By the type-omitting lemma for Π2 classes, there is a model (B,b) of ψ(c) omitting all of them. Consequently every universal type realized in (B,b) is ∃-supported; by the A5⇒A1 part of Theorem 1.2, (B,b) is ∃-atomic. Since B≅A, transporting b through an isomorphism gives a finite parameter tuple over which A is ∃-atomic. B1⇔B3 is the boldface relativization of the Ash-Knight-Manasse-Slaman and Chisholm theorem: relative computable categoricity is equivalent to having a c.e. Scott family of existential formulas over finitely many parameters. Relativizing all computations and the enumeration of the parameterized Scott family above a base oracle C gives the cone formulation; conversely, computable categoricity uniformly throughout a cone yields such a parameterized existential Scott family relative to the cone base.
